summary: This post provides a comprehensive technical deep-dive into battery technology, prompted by Tesla's 2020 Battery Day announcement. Frazelle explains the fundamentals of how batteries work—from the chemistry of redox reactions to the differences between alkaline and lithium-ion cells—before exploring emerging technologies like solid-state batteries, nuclear batteries, and silicon anodes. The bulk of the post analyzes Tesla's holistic approach to battery improvement, which addresses cell design, manufacturing processes, materials selection, and vehicle integration to achieve their goal of halving cost per kilowatt-hour.
key_points: Tesla's tabless battery design reduces electrical path length from 250mm to 50mm, enabling better thermal performance and simpler manufacturing

Silicon anodes can store 9x more lithium ions than graphite but swell 3-4x during charging, causing fractures—Tesla's solution uses raw silicon with elastic polymer coatings at $1.20/kWh vs competitors' $6-100+/kWh
Tesla's dry electrode process eliminates the solvent step entirely, resulting in 10x reduction in factory footprint and energy consumption
Making the battery pack structural (like aircraft fuel tanks in wings) reduces body mass by 10% and increases range by 14%
The combination of all improvements—cell design, factory, anode, cathode, and integration—achieves Tesla's goal to halve cost per kWh
money_quotes: What a fantastic world we live in that we can witness the first Apple-like keynote for batteries.
With silicon, the cookie crumbles and gets gooey.
It is really a huge pain in the ass to have tabs from a production standpoint.

If you take a look at the 'it's a small world journey' of I am a Nickel atom and what happens to me, it's crazy, you're going around the world three times, there is a moral equivalent of digging the ditch, filling in the ditch, and digging the ditch again. It's total madness.
The level of thought and detail put into rethinking old processes from first principles to make them more efficient is inspiring.
There is a clear 'why' for every decision made that boils down to economics, not just technical gains.
key_insight: Tesla's battery improvements succeed because they take a holistic, first-principles approach—optimizing cell design, manufacturing, materials, and vehicle integration together rather than focusing on a single breakthrough technology.
